questions.question: What is the main idea behind comparing fanout patterns across products?
options: To decide where work should happen based on workload shape instead of copying one slogan everywhere.
To prove push fanout is always the best design.
To avoid discussing user-visible freshness entirely.
To remove caches from backend systems.
questions.explanation: Fanout is fundamentally about work placement under specific constraints.

questions.question: Which workload signal most strongly warns that pure push fanout may be dangerous?
options: Large audience size combined with strong skew toward a few producers.
Low average request header size.
A small number of total microservices.
The existence of any cache in the architecture.
questions.explanation: Skew and recipient count are what create explosive amplification.

questions.question: Why is pull fanout often attractive for celebrity-style workloads?
options: It avoids exploding write work when one event would otherwise fan out to massive audiences.
It guarantees lower read latency than precomputed views.
It eliminates the need for ranking or caching.
It prevents partial degradation entirely.
questions.explanation: Pull defers work and avoids impossible write amplification.

questions.question: What is the hidden cost of choosing pull fanout too broadly?
options: Read-time merge and ranking work can become too expensive or too slow.
Recipient sets can no longer be stored durably.
Freshness becomes impossible to observe.
Queues are no longer allowed in the architecture.
questions.explanation: Pull reduces writes by shifting cost to reads.

questions.question: Which metric most directly captures fanout write amplification?
options: Number of downstream writes or enqueues per logical source event.
Average avatar image width.
The total number of dashboards in Grafana.
Median OAuth token length.
questions.explanation: Amplification should be measured close to the actual extra work created.

questions.question: What trade-off comes with mixed fanout strategies?
options: They fit skewed workloads better but add policy and debugging complexity.
They remove all need for observability.
They force every read to be stale.
They guarantee lower cost than any simpler design.
questions.explanation: Hybrids are often right, but they are not free.

questions.question: Why should fanout strategy be tied to degraded-mode planning?
options: Because delayed or failed secondary work can change whether users see stale, partial, or unavailable results.
Because degraded modes only matter for databases.
Because fanout has no effect on freshness.
Because fallback makes metrics unnecessary.
questions.explanation: Work placement determines where and how the product degrades.

questions.question: What is the cleanest reason to migrate from a pure push feed to a hybrid model?
options: Write amplification and queue lag for high-follower accounts are becoming the primary scaling risk.
Offset pagination is unpopular with frontend teams.
The company wants fewer Go services.
The cache hit rate is already perfect.
questions.explanation: A migration should respond to the dominant scaling pain, not fashion.
